summary: Gruber reviews Simplenote, a third-party iPhone notes app he's been using for eight months, arguing it succeeds by studying what makes Apple's built-in Notes app good and then improving on its key shortcomings. The main improvement is over-the-air syncing via a web app, which Gruber considers an order of magnitude better than USB or local Wi-Fi syncing. He contrasts web-based and client-to-client syncing strategies, acknowledging the privacy trade-off but concluding convenience wins. He also praises the app's use of Helvetica over Notes' Marker Felt font. Despite costing only $2, Gruber considers it possibly his favorite third-party iPhone app, though he worries the low price may not sustain the service long-term.
key_points: Simplenote succeeds because its developers clearly studied Apple's Notes app and improved on its weaknesses rather than ignoring its strengths
Over-the-air web-based syncing is vastly superior to USB or Wi-Fi client-to-client syncing because data stays current everywhere without manual intervention
Web-based syncing involves a privacy trade-off — your data lives on someone else's server — but the convenience and universal access are worth it
The $2 price point is remarkably low for an app that includes ongoing web service access, raising concerns about the developer's long-term sustainability
Good software design means understanding the hierarchy of user needs: syncing reliability and always-current data matter more than feature richness
money_quotes: For me at least, over-the-air Internet syncing is an order of magnitude more appealing than USB or Bonjour syncing.
I've tried a slew of iPhone note editing apps, and not only is Simplenote my favorite, it might be my favorite third-party iPhone app, period.
Cloud Factory clearly studied what is good about Notes and thought about how to make something that is good in the same ways, but improves upon its major shortcomings.
The Simplenote web and iPhone apps were designed to work with each other. They are of a piece.
It's a trade-off between (a) convenience and universal access and (b) the privacy advantages of your data residing only on your own devices.
What gives me pause about the low price is that I want Cloud Factory to thrive and for them to be able to maintain the web-based Simplenote component for the foreseeable future.
It's hard for me to imagine how you could get more for $2 than you will by buying Simplenote.
key_insight: The best third-party apps don't ignore platform conventions — they study what the platform maker got right and then systematically improve on what it got wrong.
